claim: Aluminum acts as a ferrite stabilizer, maintaining iron in the α‑state from ordinary temperature to the melting point and suppressing the Ar3 point.
evidence_text: If the curve of cooling of the alloy containing 5% aluminum is examined, and compared with that of an extra soft steel containing nearly the same percentage of carbon, it will be seen that the point ar3 is wanting. The aluminum thus maintains the iron, for the most part, in the a‑state from the ordinary temperature to the meltingpoint.

claim: The density of binary Fe‑Al alloys decreases monotonically with increasing aluminum content, from 7.25 g/cm³ at 6.0 wt.% Al to 6.55 g/cm³ at 12.5 wt.% Al.
Hadfield patented a group of steels in 1887 containing from 0.1 to 20 wt.% Al and later showed that aluminum had a favorable but slight effect on tensile testing and none on hardness testing.
evidence_text: the distinguished Robert A. Hadfield2 patented a group of steels in 1887 containing from 0.1 to 20 Al wt.%. ... Hadfield showed that the effect of aluminum was favorable but slight in tensile testing and none on hardness testing.

claim: Adding 5.6 wt.% aluminum to an iron‑carbon alloy reduces its melting point by only 25 °C.
evidence_text: A reduction of 25 C for an alloy including 5.6 wt.% aluminum was mentioned.

claim: Aluminum reduces steel density through a combination of its lower atomic mass and its effect in expanding the lattice parameter of iron alloys, but high Al additions lead to ordered phases (B2 and/or D0₃) that limit Al content and cause brittleness.
evidence_text: This effect results from the combination of the lower atomic mass of aluminum and the effect aluminum has in expanding the lattice parameter of iron alloys. Even though higher additions of aluminum could be desirable for weight reduction, ordered phases will appear at a certain level of aluminum (B2 and/or D0₃). This phenomenon will limit the amount of aluminum because the presence of these ordered phases will render the material brittle.

claim: Thermo-Calc density calculation for the quaternary Fe-0.2C-8.5Mn-Al system, using the TCFE7 database and phase fractions of austenite and ferrite at room temperature, shows excellent agreement at lower Al contents but deviates at higher Al contents where the κ-phase may be present.
evidence_text: The Thermo-Calc calculation (Thermo-Calc Software, Inc., McMurray, PA, USA) was performed using the database with the latest assessment by Lindahl and Selleby19 with volume data from the commercial database Thermo-Calc Software TCFE7 Steels/Fe-alloys database version 7. In this calculation, the phase fractions of austenite and ferrite were calculated in the same range of temperatures used for preparing the experimental alloys. The density was then evaluated with these phase fractions at room temperature. Agreement is excellent at the lower aluminum contents, whereas at higher aluminium contents the κ-phase, which is not considered in this calculation, might be present at these temperatures and could explain the difference with the experimental data.

claim: At high Al levels, cementite is replaced by the ordered carbide κ-phase, which has a perovskite structure (E21) and a composition approximated as (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ with incomplete C occupancy.
evidence_text: Another particularity of the addition of high levels of Al is the replacement of cementite by an ordered carbide, the κ-phase. This phase has a perovskite structure, designated as E21 in the Strukturbericht classification, but with an uncompleted occupancy of the C atom. The formula commonly used is the (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ even though results from the literature differ from this formula, mainly in the case of the ternary system Fe-Al-C and the relation between Fe and Al.

claim: The first CALPHAD modeling of the Fe-Al-C system by Hari Kumar and Raghavan used a fixed average composition of Fe₃AlC₀.₅₆₅ for the κ-phase.
evidence_text: They applied the compound energy formalism24 for the solution phases. Even though they recognized that the κ-phase presents a range of compositions they used an average fixed composition of Fe₃AlC₀.₅₆₅.

claim: Ohtani et al. introduced first-principles calculations into CALPHAD modeling to compute the formation energy of the κ-phase (Fe₃AlC) and used a model with two substitutional sublattices for ordered bcc phases.
evidence_text: Ohtani et al.25 introduced first-principles calculations into CALPHAD modeling to calculate the formation energy of the κ-phase (Fe₃AlC) and proceeded with the CALPHAD approach taking as experimental model the work of Palm and Inden.11 A model with two substitutional sublattices and one interstitial sublattice was used for the description of the ordered phases for the body-centered cubic (bcc) structures and the κ-phase was modeled with another model also with two substitutional sublattices.

claim: Connétable et al. reassessed the Fe-Al-C system with a new CALPHAD description of the κ-phase based on an order–disorder model for the fcc phase with four substitutional sublattices and carbon content restricted to 20 mol.% C.
evidence_text: They proposed a new CALPHAD description of the κ-phase based on their order–disorder model for the face-centered cubic (fcc) phase with four substitutional sublattices but restricted the carbon contents to 20 mol.% C.

claim: Lindahl and Selleby improved the Al-Fe-Mn system description using the latest assessments of the Al-Fe and Al-Mn binaries, and the complete system now well reproduces fcc-bcc equilibria in the Fe-rich corner.
evidence_text: Lindahl and Selleby19 have very recently improved the Al-Fe-Mn system description by using the latest assessments of the Al-Fe and Al-Mn binaries. The complete system, including extensive data on fcc-bcc equilibria in the Fe-rich corner, is now well reproduced.

claim: Isoplethal sections of an Fe-8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) alloy calculated with three different Hallstedt-based databases (Fig. 2) show discrepancies: the first database replaces cementite with M₅C₂ at low temperatures unsupported by experiment, and there is no agreement on the fcc+bcc+κ three-phase domain critical for continuous annealing.
evidence_text: Figure 2 shows a comparison of isoplethal sections calculated with these three databases. In the first database, cementite is replaced at relatively low temperatures by M₅C₂, which is not supported by the experiment. There are differences between the databases regarding the limits of composition of the different phase fields. For instance there is no agreement on the three-phase domain fcc + bcc + κ, which is of primary importance during continuous annealing.

claim: Austenite phase fractions for the Fe-8Mn-0.2C alloy calculated with the three databases (Fig. 3) show similar results between the first and third versions but are shifted in temperature, and the latest assessment is believed to provide the best results.
evidence_text: Figure 3 shows a comparison of austenite phase fractions using again this three databases. Similar results are observed between the first and the third version but are shifted in temperature. Nevertheless, it is believed that the latest assessment should provide the best results.

claim: In a ferritic Fe-8 wt.% Al alloy, hot torsion between 900 °C and 1100 °C at strain rates of 0.1–10 s⁻¹ revealed discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) at high temperature and strain rate, and continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) at lower temperature and strain rate.
evidence_text: The tests performed in the temperature range from 900°C to 1100°C with a strain rate range of 0.1–10 s⁻¹ showed that two types of dynamic recrystallization may operate during hot deformation: At a high temperature and high strain rate, this alloy undergoes discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX), whereas at a lower temperature and low strain rate, continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) occurs (Fig. 6).

claim: Figure 5 provides EBSD images of typical microstructures of the three high-Al steel families: ferritic, austenite-ferrite (austenite in red), and three-phase austenite-ferrite with nanoscale κ-phase (ferrite in blue, κ not visible).
evidence_text: Fig. 5. Typical microstructures of three families of high-Al steels: (a) ferritic, (b) two-phase austenite (red)-ferrite, and (c) three-phase austenite-ferrite(blue). The j-phase is not observed here due to its size. The last two images are electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) images.

claim: To avoid formation of brittle β-Mn, Mn content added to stabilize austenite should be less than 35 wt.%; C contributes to austenite stabilization and acts as a strong strengthener.
evidence_text: Mn added to stabilize austenite should be less than 35% to avoid the formation of brittle β-Mn. C that contributes also to austenite stabilization is proven to be a strong strengthener for this alloy system.

claim: In a 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C alloy solution-treated at 850°C for 10 min and water quenched, ferrite initially contains a mixture of B2 and DO3 ordered phases, as indicated by intensity differences between {111} and {200} superlattice reflections (Figure 9a).
evidence_text: The difference of intensity between {111} reflections and {200} reflections indicates that the initial ferrite structure is a mix of B2- and DO3-ordered structures.

claim: After aging the same alloy at 550°C for 1 h, the ferrite becomes fully ordered DO3, with no intensity difference between {111} and {200} reflections (Figure 9b).
evidence_text: After aging for 1 h at 550°C (Fig. 9b), there is no more intensity difference between {111} reflections and {200} reflections on the diffraction pattern; ferrite becomes a fully ordered DO3 structure.

claim: Increasing annealing temperature and/or time decreases tensile strength and yield strength; Al and Si serve as important solid-solution strengtheners (Figure 10).
evidence_text: Increasing the annealing temperature and/or time leads to a decrease in tensile strength with the yield strength behaving in a similar way. ... The influence of Al and Si, both important solid-solution strengtheners, is also observed.

claim: At 800°C annealing, ferrite recrystallization is incomplete with pancake-shaped grains, and austenite is either transformed to martensite or heavily dislocated (confirmed by TEM); at 1000°C, ferrite is fully recrystallized (Figure 11).
evidence_text: recrystallization of ferrite is not complete at 800°C in comparison with that at 1000°C (Fig. 11a and b). Ferrite grains in the shape of pancakes are mainly observed at the lower annealing temperatures. ... In the sample annealed at 800°C, austenite is not easily indexed and this could be due either to a transformation of austenite into martensite or to a heavily dislocated austenite. The latter was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy studies.

claim: In a finite-element front crash simulation, replacing DP600 with the austenite-ferrite steel reduced the front side member mass from 5.88 kg to 4.63 kg (21% saving) and with the fully austenitic steel to 3.99 kg (32% saving).
evidence_text: Thickness Fr / Rr (mm) 1.7 / 2.1 1.5 / 1.8 1.3 / 1.7 Total Mass / vehicle (kg) 5.88 4.63 3.99 Weight saving / vehicle kg - 1.25 1.89 % - 21 % 32 %

claim: For the A-Pillar, the mass per vehicle was 6.11 kg for DP600, 4.84 kg for the austenite-ferrite steel, and 4.12 kg for the fully austenitic steel, representing weight savings of 1.27 kg (21%) and 1.99 kg (33%).
evidence_text: Thickness (mm) 1.6 1.4 1.25 Total Mass / vehicle (kg) 6.11 4.84 4.12 Weight saving / vehicle kg - 1.27 1.99 % - 21 % 33 %
